    3. Finding Your Message | 35 Famous Creatives And Their Message
</h2>

  <!-- Attachment Blocks -->
<!-- DEBUG: attachments.count=2 -->

  <!-- DEBUG: attachment.id=33605780, kind=text -->
  <div class="lecture-attachment lecture-attachment-type-text" id="lecture-attachment-33605780">
    <div class="attachment-data"></div>
    
      <div class="attachment-data"></div>
<div class="lecture-text-container">
<p></p><p>If you‘re not interested in clout chasing...</p><p>Or selling your body on Onlyfans.com...</p><p>If instead, you want the focus on the value of your art... </p><p>Then the best "shortcut" I know is:</p><p><strong>Make the reason why you do what you do very clear to the world. So they can latch on.</strong></p><p>And you do that by having an irresistible message.</p><p>The process simplified is this...</p><p>First. Find your 'what': IE. Party Photographer</p><p>Then. Find your 'how' (your unique way of doing your "what"): IE. Take the craziest photos at parties, not just people standing there.</p><p>And then... You lead every bit of communication with your "why" (the reason you do "what" you do "how" you do it): IE. Party photos are boring. Let's create a world where regular people look just as glamorous as celebrities -- and create photos so "real" that you can hear the music in the room just by looking at them. </p><p>Your "what" and the "how" are about you.</p><p>Your "why" is about your audience.</p><p>Your "why" is your message.</p><p>Your message is a description of the NEW WORLD that you want to take your "family of fans" to.</p><p>It's the journey you're taking them on.</p><p>World-class artists who have managed to emerge from the shadows have one thing in common:</p><p>They have a message.</p><p>A great message succinctly describes the vision you have for a new way of seeing the world.</p><p>A message is not a slogan.</p><p>A message describes your unique vision of the world and implies what you and I have to do and think in order to get there.</p><p>A message is not a slogan.</p><p>A message shows the gap between where we are now and where we need to be.</p><p><strong>For example: "Black Lives Matter" is a slogan (an expression of the new world order).</strong></p><p><strong>But a slogan without a message is powerless. It doesn't give people a plan of action or a new world to aspire to.</strong></p><p><strong>"It's time to create a world that's rid of systemic racism" is the BLM message.</strong></p><p><strong>It's this message that inspires people into action.</strong></p><p><strong>The message tells people where you're taking them to.</strong></p><p><strong>The slogan... It's just shorthand for the message.</strong></p><p>No-one is interested in a vision of the world that already exists.</p><p>A great message is not about you. It’s about making better lives for other people.</p><p>YOUR MESSAGE IS BIGGER THAN YOU.</p><p>You may be the originator of the message, but the purpose of a great message is to free OTHER PEOPLE from their old ways of thinking. It takes us all into a new world. Just like the creative people you love did for you at some point.</p><p>We're all trying to make our lives better.</p><p>Every one of us.</p><p>That's why people who have a message are so crucial to society. They may not have everything figured out, but at least they have a proposed path out of one aspect of the current situation. To make our lives better.</p><p>A great message makes people feel less alone. Tell us: "What is the new world you're bringing us to?"</p><p>The path may be as ambitious as "healing the environment" or as subtle as "a new way of painting".</p><p>A great message is a call to enhance, make something old new again, to birth or to destroy. </p><p>It's what artists mean when they say they "wanna bring the culture forward". </p><p>That's why most great messages are by nature contrarian. They are about a refusal to go along with the accepted way of doing things.</p><p>We love artist who provide a blueprint for a new world. And the more people these artists find that are sympathetic to their vision, the more we watch these artists rise and rise.</p><p>Along the way, other artists may align themselves with their movement to help bring this message forward.</p><p>Maybe you share a message (a yet unrealized vision of the world) with another artist out there?  </p><p>The goal is to get people to accept, agree and adopt your new vision of the world.</p><p>And once they do.</p><p>Everyone else just copies.</p><p><br></p><h2>Assembly Line Creatives</h2><p>If you're working in the area of film, photography, music, art and fashion etc... and you're creating work piggybacking on someone else's breakthrough message without adding your unique voice to it, then you're just an "assembly line creative".</p><p>"Assembly line creatives" copy the basic aesthetic of someone's message without trying to further the intention behind it.</p><p>Strong breakthrough messages create a cottage industry for assembly line creatives to follow in their footsteps.</p><p>You may be jumping on a bandwagon. But you do provide a service.</p><p>Lucky you... You can clock-in and clock-out. There are no stakes in your journey. Your life is comfortable.</p><p>Which is fine.</p><p>But that also makes you more vulnerable because you're interchangeable with all the other assembly line creatives out there. Because what you’re doing isn't special or inspiring (since it doesn't have your unique voice in it and is only copying the surface). It doesn't bring a community or individuals around it. People don't feel passion for this kind of "art". It's just to keep boredom away (until someone comes around with an actual message).</p><p>But don't get me wrong. There is tremendous value in keeping boredom away.</p><p>But The Mystery School wasn't created for assembly line creatives. </p><p><em>The Mystery School was designed for the kind of artists who want to wake people up.</em></p><p><br></p><h2><strong>Successful People With No Message</strong></h2><p>"But Bronques," you may ask, "What about all those lame-ass "successful" people you see that have no message? Surely Jake Paul doesn't have a message, right? And Kim Kardashian doesn't have a message, right? Why even bother with a message?"</p><p>Not so fast, tiger. Most of them do. You just forgot. Or... You weren't really paying attention while they were making their mark-- to notice. (I'll show you their message below.)</p><p>If you try to analyze the message of someone that's already risen out of obscurity... It may leave you confused. Their fame seems so random. But believe me, it's not.</p><p>Before they were on your radar, there was a reason people were flocking to their side. All you saw was the aftermath of that success. And now that they're famous, they can afford to expand into other avenues. Everything they do now has the context of their current success. They are "post-message". </p><p><br></p><h2><strong>Origin Story</strong></h2><p>But if you really want to understand what made these people stick out, you need to go back to their origin story. That is where you will find their breakthrough message.</p><p>This is a really useful exercise because YOU are at the beginning of YOUR origin story.</p><p>Like <span style="color: rgb(0, 0, 0);">Jon Acuff once said “Don't compare your beginning to someone else's middle.”</span></p><p>So I've collected a few examples of the breakthrough messages that led artists out of obscurity in the past. Hopefully their messages will inspire you to get closer to yours. </p><p>And remember, while these messages may seem “obvious” now, at the time they were radical! </p><p>Remember life before The Weeknd? (I know you don't) The most cutting edge R&B at the time was a movement called "neo- soul". Artist like Maxwell, D'Angelo and Erica Badu had a smooth jazzy mid-tempo sound. Well, guess what? A Canadian artist and his team of producers made that style of R&B obsolete overnight with a radical message (we'll it discuss below) that is the status quo now.</p><p><br></p><h2><strong>Enhance, Bring Back, Birth Or Destroy</strong></h2><p>That's what all the best messages do. </p><p>They enhance, bring shit back, birth or destroy.</p><p>If you've been behaving like a Assembly Line Creative for the last few years, it's time to get out of riding in the same lane as everyone else.</p><p>How? </p><p>Tell us what you want to "enhance, bring back, birth or destroy".</p><p>Sometimes a message negates everything that came before it. </p><p>Sometimes it only seeks to carve out a new space in the world that will co-exist with everything else.</p><p>Sometimes it takes over the culture, because that's what the culture needed.</p><p>But a message is always an agent of change.</p><p>Study those who came before you. You'll see. No matter who the new artist is. If they’re connect with masses of people, it's because they have a message.</p><p>People only pay attention to what is “special”. There's no other way to make people pay attention to what you're doing. Unless it's your parents or your bestie...</p><p>Your message is what will make complete strangers pay attention.</p><p>You can fool people with a new haircut, or some clever PR antics, but your message is what will keep people focused on you after all the noise dies down.</p><p><br></p><h2><strong>Exceptions</strong></h2><p>But before we dive in, let me mention the exceptions:</p><p>If you’re "ridiculously good-looking"...</p><p>Or you were born with a 1 in a billion talent or physical attribute...</p><p>Or ended up in the right place at the right time (LUCK!)...</p><p>Then you don’t need a message (or some might argue that this IS your message).</p><p>I hope you have the foresight to cash in your "free pass" and the 15 minutes of fame you were handed... And quickly parlay this or another more profound part of yourself into a message. </p><p>You know... Just in case people decide to move on to the next "freak show" or "platter-du-jour" one day. </p><p>For these exceptions... Cash in on OnlyFans and YouTube!</p><p>Everyone else? </p><p>You need a message.</p><p><br></p><h2><strong>Classic Messages</strong></h2><p>The most universal and powerful message that you can have as an artist is to bring forth a unique art / music / film / fashion style that no one has experienced before. It typically only happens every 10 years, but if you invent a new form of music, or painting, film-making, or photography concept that everyone else copies... That IS enough of a message. You have profoundly affected the world. Congratulations. I love you.</p><h2>What about the world would you like to change with your art?</h2><p>World doesn't have to mean the planet.</p><p>It could be the artworld.</p><p>It could be the music business.</p><p>It could be the publishing industry or The Austin Slam poetry community.</p><p>It could be your own little community of surfboard designers.</p><p>It should be whatever 'world' you're passionate about.</p><h2>'Excellence' is it's own message.</h2><p>But I'm not talking about “you're really good at something" kind of excellence. I'm talking about G.O.A.T. levels of excellence. The "absolute best that the planet has ever known" kind of excellence.  </p><p>The most universal and powerful message that you can spread as a human being is the "be true to yourself / live life to the fullest" message. But you don't get to merely say it. You have to embody it every day you're alive in order for it to become your message. People that are truly themselves without fear of consequence don't need any other message than their mere existance to succeed. Their middle finger to the inner and outer critic is the ultimate message. A message like this is all you need to propel yourself to the top of whatever sector you're working in. While everyone else has their half-inspired "I'm gonna be myself from now on" moments every few months... These creative human forces of nature live it 24/7/365. That's why we love them. They are a rare, rare breed.</p><p>The "other" most universal powerful message that you can spread as a human being is to "love without boundaries". It's the Jesus Christ inspired message. But unless you're gonna take it to that Jesus Christ inspired level (like Ghandi did), it isn't a really a message you're living. You're living a slogan. You're just a "weekend warrior" like everyone else living with their ups and downs, and their loves and hates. </p><p>People think Jesus's message was "Love everybody." It wasn't. That's not groundbreaking. "Love thy enemy." was his message. That's the new way of thinking he was trying to take the world to. And that's why he was crucified. </p><p>Hey, I never said this was gonna be easy.</p><p><br></p><h1><strong>HERE'S A LIST OF 35 ARTISTS YOU PROBABLY KNOW AND THE MESSAGE THAT BROKE THEM OUT OF OBSCURITY</strong></h1><p><br></p><h2><strong><em>These are just 35 ideas to get you started.</em></strong></h2><p><br></p><p><strong>RESTAURANTS</strong></p><p><br></p><p>Noma (#1 restaurant in the world): The highest form of culinary experience is when all the ingredients are in season and harvested from your own backyard. Let's start a restaurant that showcases this concept and inspire others all around the world to apply it in their own way.</p><p><br></p><p>Anthony Bourdin: The best way to experience culture and live life to the fullest is through food. Let's inspire people to adopt that attitude when they travel too.</p><p><br></p><p><strong>FILM</strong></p><p><br></p><p>(A film without a message always feels wrong.)</p><p><br></p><p>Tarantino: Dialogue is music. I want to make movies where THAT concept is the centerpiece of this visual medium. </p><p><br></p><p>Aaron Sorkin: Dialogue is music. I want to write screenplays where THAT concept the centerpiece of this visual medium. (Same message expressed in a completely different way than Tarantino)</p><p><br></p><p>Christopher Nolan: A blockbuster can have as many deep, meaningful, intelligent and complex themes as an iNdie film can. (the whole DNA of what he stands for is in his very first film 'Memento')</p><p><br></p><p><strong>YOUTUBE</strong></p><p><br></p><p>Jake Paul: Every single day that you're alive is exciting, you just have to find the hidden gems every day. (And then he created a YouTube channel to prove it. His slogan says it all: "It's every day bro.")</p><p><br></p><p><strong>FASHION</strong></p><p><br></p><p>Chanel: Let's make elegance accessible to ALL women.</p><p><br></p><p>Kardashian women believed in a new kind of feminine empowerment... "Let's start a television show where we show girls that you can be glamorous AND TOTALLY REAL. We will glorify thick eyebrows, thick asses, be show ourselves at our messiest and still be proud (the "women that run with the wolves" message) . They had this message at a time when NONE of those things were even conceivable in the culture. You had to be skinny, have thin eyebrows and it wasn't cool to be in a reality show and show your messy life "warts and all". Now that's everything a legion of girls are aspiring to be.</p><p><br></p><p>American Apparel: The "girl next door" wearing "girls next door" clothing... is sexier than porn stars are. </p><p><br></p><p>Terry Richardson: Fashion photography should be raw and sexy and shot with a point-and-shoot. If you have an eye, don't let yourself be limited.</p><p><br></p><p><strong>MUSIC</strong></p><p><br></p><p>So you thinkg good music doesn't exist anymore? OK boomer.</p><p><br></p><p>Every generation's "new haircuts" usually come with a brand new sound, and THAT combo will always be an irresistible message to the youth.</p><p><br></p><p>If you're fortunate to have invented a whole new sound and style, then that's all the message you need. Unfortunately, these days, many musicians think that using a different snare sample is a "new sound". But sorry bro, it takes more than that. In the end, the fans will be the judge.</p><p><br></p><p>The Weeknd: The Weeknd’s first love is film. So when he started making music, he decided to create a new kind of cinematic R&B. Songs with darker lyrics and a darker sound, bringing his audience of loners and party girls into a different world every time.</p><p><br></p><p>Skrillex: Dubstep is the new punk/pop (and that's saying a lot since he started his career in a punk/pop band). </p><p><br></p><p>Lady Gaga: Let's bring fashion and outrageous "pomp" back into music (just like Queen -- where her name was inspired from)</p><p><br></p><p>Billie Eilish: new haircut + new homegrown sound. "You don't have to be sexy be successful. You don't have to be loud either." This feminist message comes back every couple of years when the pendulum is swinging too far the other side for women.</p><p><br></p><p>Kanye: a new sound: "high pitch samples" sound. + post-message: Rappers can be preppy instead of gangster. This guy... Love him, or hate him... But he brings forth a new "post-message" and sound every few years! And other assembly line creatives just copy his scraps. He even managed to shake the political world with his "The president doesn't care about black people." message. That's why I use 'The Kanye Test' in The Drill Down Method.</p><p><br></p><p><strong>WRITING</strong></p><p><br></p><p>Rupi Kaur: You can use Instagram to share unapologetically personal and sensitive poetry -- words instead of just selfies. </p><p><br></p><p>Steven King: "Scary stories" are serious art. Just as much as classic literature is.</p><p><br></p><p><strong>BRANDS</strong></p><p><br></p><p>Onlyfans: The "girl next door" is sexier than the porn star.</p><p><br></p><p>Nike: Physical excellence and activism go hand in hand in the quest for greatness.</p><p><br></p><p><strong>PODCASTS</strong></p><p><br></p><p>Joe Rogan: Interviews shouldn't be limited to 30 minute or 1 hour boxes. + "post-message": Leader of the Anti-woke culture</p><p><br></p><p>Carmela Habibi (my friend isn't famous yet, but she's an example of what I think is the future, when a hot body will be so common, it won't be enough, a new breed of Amazonian "women who run with the wolves" will emerge): Intelligent Thots (the name of her podcast is everything she stands for - just look at her Instagram if you want proof)</p><p><br></p><p><em>Father Bronques (Make Art Not Content): A culture that worships image over substance is toxic. It’s up to artists and creatives to restore some sanity to the world by outsmarting the content makers and releasing ART drenched in authenticity and depth. Let's make pure creativity the new religion. </em></p><p><br></p><p><strong>PERSONALITIES</strong></p><p><br></p><p>Muhammad Ali: Physical excellence and activism go hand in hand in the quest for greatness. He made that clear when he changed his name from Cassius Clay and protested the Vietnam war. That's when Muhammad Ali was born, NOT when he won his first fight. </p><p><br></p><p>Anthony Robbins: Just like athletes need coaches, regular people need personal coaches too to navigate through life.</p><p><br></p><p>Oprah: Let’s make the "self help movement" cool by using the talk show format to make these ideas popular. Post-message: Book clubs aren't only for nerds.</p><p><br></p><p><strong>INSTAGRAM</strong></p><p><br></p><p>Caroline Calloway: "Girls, let's turn oversharing into an asset, a weapon, and a source of income." </p><p><br></p><p>@sarahbahbah: Why not memeify our own lives as a source for our art and creative direction and glamorize our overthinking minds in the process? Let's put a spotlight on our truth.</p><p><br></p><p><strong>ART</strong></p><p><br></p><p>Basquiat: African art deserves a respected place in the history of art. </p><p><br></p><p>Warhol: Everything around you is art. Let's make art accessible to everybody. </p><p><br></p><p>Dali: The language of dreams reveal the ultimate truths. + "Post-message": Your life is the real work of art.</p><p><br></p><p>Bob Ross: The therapeutic power of making art should be available to everybody."So he made a TV show to show people it was possible.</p><p><br></p><p><strong>POLITICS</strong></p><p><br></p><p>Trump: Fuck “presidential”. Entrepreneur spirit is what gets things done.</p><p><br></p><p>Hillary Clinton's message: SHE HAD NO MESSAGE. THAT'S WHY SHE LOST.</p><p><br></p><p>Emma Gonzalez: If older people don't have the energy to change things, the youth will . Slogan: "I call B.S."</p><p><br></p><p>Martin Luther King: The "I have a dream..." speech is his message. When your message is so powerful, so clear, so good and so controversial... That it gets you killed. One of the strongest messages ever.</p><p><br></p><p>P.S. <span style="color: rgb(0, 0, 0);">A mystery student sent me their "message" the other day. I noticed a problem it had that's pretty common when people are on the message quest. Read this... And then put yours to the test: Messages are not about YOU. They're about where you want to take your community, your people, or the world. One “message” I hear all the time is “I wanna help people.” Ok sure, that's great... but that’s not a message. But HOW you wanna help people though... THAT might be the beginning to exposing the message lurking below your surface. For example, an artist who says: “I think patients would benefit if there was more art up in hospitals. I’m going to change that by curating artists to donate art... starting with my local hospital.” Now, that first sentence is a message. It shows us a simple new vision of the world you wanna bring us to. And by default-- it helps people. That's a message a community can get behind (or rally against). So let me clarify this again: A message is not about you. It’s about other people. It’s about freeing people from their old ways of thinking (like a new genre of music does). It’s about taking people to a new place (like creating a society where law enforcement respects minorities). You may end up being one of the prime benefactors of your message... But even if you do... Ultimately, it ain’t about you.</span></p><p></p>
</div>
